A blaze scorched about 200 acres of dry molasses grass and slash pine in a remote area of the Na Pali-Kona Forest Reserve on the island of Kauai.  The cause of the fire was unknown. It had burned only 40 acres through the end of May.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the islands generated surf of 15 to 25 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, and Molokai; and the north-facing shores of Oahu and Maui; and 10 to 15 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and north-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii.  At least three individuals required assistance by paramedics after getting into trouble in the surf.  Lifeguards also performed many rescues and provided warnings to beach goers to stay away from the water.  There were no reports of serious property damage.

The fire came within 25 yards of homes in the area, and nearby residents were affected by thick smoke and ash.  However, there were no serious injuries or property damage.  Fire officials believe the blaze was intentionally set.

An upper air low northwest of the Garden Isle of Kauai generated heavy showers and thunderstorms over and adjacent to the island.  The rain caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  Lightning apparently knocked a radio station off the air for a time, and the inclement conditions forced airlines to delay and cancel several flights to and from the Lihue airport.  There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

An upper low east of the state brought high winds to the upper elevations in Maui and on the Big Island of Hawaii.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

A blaze scorched more than 40 acres of dry molasses grass and slash pine in a remote area of the Na Pali-Kona Forest Reserve on the island of Kauai.  The cause of the fire was unknown.  It did not threaten any structures or rare tropical vegetation.  No significant property damage or injuries were reported.  However, the blaze remained active through the end of the month.

A fire burned around 1200 acres of dry brush in Waianae and Lualualei valleys in leeward Oahu.  The blaze also destroyed a hale used as a classroom at Kaala Farms Cultural Center and a few orchid hothouses on Haleahi Road.  Up to a dozen residents had to evacuate their homes for a time due to heavy smoke in the area.  The fire started near Radford Street and Kolekole Road at Lualualei Naval Magazine, though the cause of the fire was under investigation.  There were no significant injuries.  The cost of damages was not available.
An upper low east of the state brought high winds to the upper elevations in Maui and on the Big Island of Hawaii.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

A fire blackened 4900 acres of dry brush, and macadamia and coffee farm lands, near Pahala in the Kau District on the Big Island of Hawaii.  The fire forced the evacuation of Kau Hospital.  The 15 long-term care patients at the facility were moved for a time to the Naalehu Community Center.  Highway 11 in the area was also closed for six hours as firefighters battled the inferno.  The cause of the fire was unknown.  There were no serious injuries reported.  No damage estimates were available.  The fire was about 60% contained by the end of June.

A fire burned more than 650 acres of dry brush and eucalyptus in an area one mile west of Waimea Canyon State Park on Kauai.  Many of the eucalyptus trees caught in the fire were 80 to 120 feet tall.  The blaze was hard to fight because it was in a remote location.  No serious injuries were reported.  The cost of damages was not available, and the cause of the blaze was not known.  By the end of June, the fire was about 15% contained.

A fire burned about 10 acres of dry brush and eucalyptus in an area one mile west of Waimea Canyon State Park on Kauai.  This episode is a continuation of a fire that began on June 25.  The acreage affected in June was 650 acres, so the total area blackened was 660 acres over the two months.  No serious injuries were reported.  The cost of damages was not available, though the cost of fighting the blaze was around $475 thousand.  The cause of the fire was not known.

A fire scorched 430 acres of dry brush just north of Hina Lani Street in Kailua-Kona on the lee side of the Big Island of Hawaii.  No significant injuries or property damage were reported.  The cause of the blaze was under investigation.  The cost of any damages was not available.  The fire was listed as contained and under control by the later afternoon July 5.

A fire blackened 300 acres of dry brush, and macadamia and coffee farm lands, near Pahala in the Kau District on the Big Island of Hawaii.  This episode was a continuation of a blaze that began on June 18.  The fire had consumed 4900 acres of vegetation in June, so the final total was approximately 5200 acres. The cause of the fire was unknown.  There were no serious injuries reported.  Only partial damage estimates were available - $112 thousand loss on macadamia nuts.

A cold front moving through the island chain brought strong winds and heavy showers.  The winds downed power lines and trees, and snapped a tree branch which injured a couple of visitors from the mainland in Waikiki at the International Market Place.  The winds became very powerful at higher elevations, especially over the summit of Mauna Kea on the Big Island.  The rain produced only nuisance flooding on Oahu.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Swells from a powerful low far northwest of the islands generated surf of 15 to 25 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and along the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 18 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu; and 6 to 10 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii.  There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage, though lifeguards rendered assistance to numerous swimmers and surfers in the rough water around the island chain. Also, a report from the public indicated sand across Kamehameha Highway near Rock Piles surf break on Oahu.

A fire blackened 38 acres of dry brush on the leeward side of the Big Island of Hawaii near Kailua-Kona.  The blaze came very close to homes in the Pines neighborhood, not far from where the fire started at the Nani Kailua Drive and Hualalai Road intersection.  Many residents had to be evacuated for a time.  In the end, however, no significant property damage or injuries were reported.  The cause of the fire was undetermined.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry weather, D2 drought conditions or worse, continued to plague parts Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island of Hawaii; and all of Lanai.  Mandatory and voluntary water restrictions remained in force.

An upper low brought heavy showers and winter weather to the Big island of Hawaii.  Snow and freezing rain fell over the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and freezing rain affected the summits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface and upper troughs near the islands generated heavy precipitation across Oahu and Kauai, with flash flooding occurring over northern parts of Kauai as well.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weliweli Road, Hapa Road, and Ala Kinoki closed due to flooding in Koloa.

A fire blackened about 80 acres of dry brush near Waikoloa on the Big Island of Hawaii.  The fire started just below the Kekumu Apartments in the Paniolo Estates.  However, the fire did not threaten any property, and no injuries were reported.  The cause of the blaze was not known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the islands generated surf of 15 to 25 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, and Molokai; and the north-facing shores of Oahu and Maui; and 10 to 15 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and north-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii.  At least three individuals required assistance by paramedics after getting into trouble in the surf.  Lifeguards also performed many rescues and provided warnings to beach goers to stay away from the water.  There were no reports of serious property damage.

A series of swells from a deep low far northwest of the islands caused surf of 15 to 25 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 10 to 18 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai. Lifeguards rescued several people in trouble and warned many others to stay away from the rough surf if they were not experienced in such conditions.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

A cold front and an upper-level low combined to generate severe weather across much of the Aloha State.  Significant weather included thunderstorms, flash flooding, record-setting hail, and a tornado.  Although the total cost of damages was not available for the entire state, preliminary numbers include $2 million in infrastructure damage on Kauai and $3.2 million in Maui.  There were no reports of fatalities or serious injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow along Kuhio Highway south of the Kalihiwai Bridge closed both lanes of traffic.

Although the total cost of damages was not available for the entire state, preliminary numbers include $2 million in infrastructure damage on Kauai and $3.2 million in Maui.  There were no reports of fatalities or serious injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail about 1.75 inches in size observed by a resident near Aikahi Park in Kailua on Oahu.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers fell over the windward side of the Big Island near Papaaloa, just to the southeast of Laupahoehoe. A motorist, a tourist from California, apparently tried to cross the swollen Pahale Stream and died after being swept away by the rapid current. The vehicle he was driving was considerably damaged as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief heavy rainfall along the windward slopes of the Big Island above Papaaloa caused rapid rises in local streams.  Broadcast media reported that a tourist from California was swept to his death in the Pahale Stream while attempting to cross.  It appears the individual may have been lost while seeking a local cabin where he had reservations for the night.

Another upper low in September, moving from the northwest, produced heavy rain over parts of East Oahu.  There was ponding on roadways, and minor drainage ditch and small stream flooding.  No significant injuries or property damage were reported.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the Aloha State generated surf of 15 to 20 feet along the north-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 8 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, and Oahu.  Lifeguards made several rescues of individuals in trouble amidst the high waves.  However, no serious injuries or property damage were reported.

A fire of unknown origin blackened 500 acres of dry brush and grass in Kaena Point State Park in the northwest part of Oahu.  The blaze came within 400 yards of the Kaena Point Satellite Tracking Station, but it did not affect any of the facility's property or operations.  The cost of any damages was not available.  There were no reports of serious injuries.

A blaze broke out over a 100-acre area of dry brush and grass in western Oahu, near a bus turn-around at Haleahi and Waianae Valley roads.  Police evacuated several homes along Waianae Valley Road as a precaution.  The exact cause of the fire was not reported.  There were no significant injuries.  The cost of any damages was not available.

A fire blackened about 3000 acres of dry brush along the Pokii, Paua, and Waiaka ridges near Kekaha in leeward Kauai.  Several power poles were destroyed by the blaze, and many residents were without electricity for a time.  Water usage in the area around Kekaha and Waimea was affected, as well, because electrical power to a water storage tank was cut off.  The cost of damages was not available.  There were no serious injuries reported.  The cause of the fire was under investigation.

An upper low northwest of Kauai generated heavy rain over parts of Maui, Lanai, and Oahu.  The showers caused drainage ditch and small stream flooding, and ponding on roadways.  However, there were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of Hawaii generated surf of 15 to 25 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, and Molokai, and the north-facing shores of Maui; and 10 to 15 feet along the north-facing, and 6 to 8 feet along the west-facing, shores of the Big Island.  Lifeguards were busy during this episode preventing beach-goers from entering the dangerous surf and occasionally providing assistance to those who entered the water nonetheless.  However, no serious injuries or property damage were reported.

An area of tropical moisture moved over Oahu and helped initiate heavy showers over the central part of the isle.  The rain caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding of roadways.  However, there were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.

An upper trough near the islands induced heavy rain and thunderstorms over Maui and Oahu.  The heavy downpour with the thunderstorm on Oahu may have contributed to the partial collapse of a roof on a school's old auditorium on Friday, Nov. 23.  The rain caused significant ponding on roadways for a brief time on Oahu, and to a lesser extent Maui; and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  Also, there were several reports of small hail with the thunderstorm on Oahu.  No serious injuries were reported.  The cost of damages on the school auditorium was not available.

A combination of swells generated surf of 15 to 25 feet along the north-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island of Hawaii; 8 to 14 feet along the west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, and Molokai; and 6 to 10 feet along the east-facing shores of Oahu and the Big Island.  Lifeguards rescued several individuals who were overwhelmed by the dangerous surf, including a man in his mid-40s taken to a hospital in critical condition on Oahu after getting into trouble at Sandy Beach.  That was on Sunday, Nov. 4.  No serious property damage was reported.

A band of heavy trade showers moved over the Garden Isle of Kauai.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

A cold front and an upper-level low combined to generate severe weather across much of the Aloha State.  Significant weather included thunderstorms, flash flooding, record-setting hail, and a tornado.  Although the total cost of damages was not available for the entire state, preliminary numbers include $2 million in infrastructure damage on Kauai and $3.2 million in Maui.  There were no reports of fatalities or serious injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris flow occurred along Hana Highway at mile marker 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist flow from the south brought heavy showers to the Big Island of Hawaii and the Garden Isle of Kauai.  The rain was intense enough in Kauai to cause flash flooding.  However, there were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.
